title: Multi-Site Pollen Evidence for Pre-Maize Cultivation: Amaranthaceae Exceedance in the Eastern Agricultural Complex Heartland
abstract: The Eastern Agricultural Complex (EAC) has abundant macrobotanical evidence but virtually no regional pollen evidence for pre-maize cultivation. We apply a baseline exceedance framework to Amaranthaceae pollen from 93 eastern North American sites in the Neotoma Paleoecology Database. Amaranthaceae exceedance beyond the pre-3,000 BP baseline (mean + 2 SD) is detected at 38.7% of sites (36/93) with a 0% false-positive rate over two millennia. The earliest exceedances (3,000-2,000 BP) cluster in the archaeologically documented EAC heartland and predate maize by over a millennium. An Artemisia co-exceedance test rules out drought and wildfire as primary drivers (5.9%). European-derived indicators yield 0% exceedance at 111 ENA sites, confirming that indicator choice determines what agriculture pollen analysis can detect. RPP correction yields a corrected Ambrosia/Zea ratio of 0.84x (95% CI: 0.33-1.68x). We interpret the 38.7% signal as the first multi-site pollen evidence consistent with EAC cultivation.
novelty_statement: First multi-site pollen evidence for EAC cultivation using Amaranthaceae exceedance (38.7% of 93 sites, 0% false-positive rate). Includes Artemisia co-exceedance test, RPP Monte Carlo correction, archaeological cross-validation, and spatial null model.
